title: Kashk-e Bademjan (Eggplant & Whey Dip)
description: A rich, savory Persian appetizer made of fried eggplants mashed with onions, garlic, mint, and kashk (fermented whey), creating a deeply flavorful and creamy dip.

instructions.text: Peel the eggplants and slice them lengthwise into 1/2-inch thick pieces. Sprinkle them generously with salt and let them sit in a colander for 30 minutes to draw out the bitterness. Rinse well and pat them completely dry with paper towels.
Heat the vegetable oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat. Fry the eggplant slices in batches until golden brown and soft on both sides. Drain on paper towels.
In a separate pan, heat 2 tbsp of oil over medium heat. Add the sliced onions and slowly fry them until deeply caramelized and golden (Piaz Daagh). Remove half of the onions for garnish.
To the remaining onions in the pan, add the minced garlic, turmeric, and black pepper. Sauté for 1 minute until fragrant. Add the fried eggplants to this pan.
Add 1/2 cup of water to the pan. Cover and let the eggplants simmer on low heat for 10-15 minutes until they are extremely soft and falling apart.
Remove the lid and use a potato masher or a wooden spoon to roughly mash the eggplants until you achieve a chunky dip consistency.
Stir in the diluted kashk. Cook on low for another 5 minutes to let the flavors meld. Taste for salt (kashk is salty, so you may not need any extra).
In a small saucepan, heat 1 tbsp of oil. Add the dried mint, stir for 5-10 seconds, and immediately remove from heat to make the mint oil (Na'na Daagh).
Transfer the dip to a shallow serving bowl. Drizzle with the mint oil, extra kashk, caramelized onions, and chopped walnuts.

tips: Frying the eggplants is traditional, but you can also brush them with oil and roast them at 400°F (200°C) for 25-30 minutes for a lighter version. Do not skip salting the eggplants, as it prevents them from absorbing too much oil.
storage_notes: Store in an airtight container in the fridge for up to 4 days. Reheat gently on the stovetop or in the microwave.
serve_with: Warm Persian flatbreads like Sangak, Barbari, or Lavash, alongside fresh herbs (Sabzi Khordan).

substitutions.note: If kashk is unavailable, mix equal parts thick Greek yogurt and sour cream. It won't have the distinct fermented flavor, but it provides the right creamy texture.
